title: Why Some Plants Survive in Heat, Salt, and Drought

lesson.learn: Plants survive harsh environments by changing how they store water, move gases, and protect their tissues. In deserts, salty soils, and exposed rocky landscapes, survival depends on reducing loss while making the best use of limited resources. Some plants use thick cuticles, tiny leaves, deep roots, or water-storing stems. Others shift their metabolism so they open stomata at night, limit evaporation during the day, or tolerate salt and heat that would damage more delicate species. Those adaptations are not just survival tricks. They are the reason plants can occupy almost every climate on Earth, from dry deserts to cold mountain slopes.
lesson.simpler: Some plants are built for tough places. They save water, protect themselves from heat, and use special shapes to survive.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Plant adaptations are trade-offs that help a species conserve water, handle stress, and keep growing where conditions are difficult.
lesson.deeperDive:
Desert plants are especially good examples of evolutionary engineering. A cactus or succulent is not just "tough"; it is a package of anatomical and chemical solutions tuned for water scarcity.

Climate stress, soil chemistry, and competition all influence which adaptations matter most. That is why the same plant family can include species that look dramatically different depending on whether they live in shade, salt flats, dunes, or alpine rock.
lesson.funFact: Some succulents can expand and shrink after rain like living water tanks, which lets them ride out long dry spells.

people: Agnes Arber - Plant form and function
concepts: Xerophyte - Plant adapted to dry conditions
Root depth - Strategy for reaching moisture
